original_abstract: Although the objective of any 'omic science is broad measurement of its constituents, such coverage has been challenging in metabolomics because the metabolome is comprised of a chemically diverse set of small molecules with variable physical properties. While extensive studies have been performed to identify metabolite isolation and separation methods, these strategies introduce bias toward lipophilic or water-soluble metabolites depending on whether reversed-phase (RP) or hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) is used, respectively. Here we extend our consideration of metabolome isolation and separation procedures to integrate RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS profiling. An aminopropyl-based HILIC/MS method was optimized on the basis of mobile-phase additives and pH, followed by evaluation of reproducibility. When applied to the untargeted study of perturbed bacterial metabolomes, the HILIC method enabled the accurate assessment of key, dysregulated metabolites in central carbon pathways (e.g., amino acids, organic acids, phosphorylated sugars, energy currency metabolites), which could not be retained by RPLC. To demonstrate the value of the integrative approach, bacterial cells, human plasma, and cancer cells were analyzed by combined RPLC/HILIC separation coupled to ESI positive/negative MS detection. The combined approach resulted in the observation of metabolites associated with lipid and central carbon metabolism from a single biological extract, using 80% organic solvent (ACN:MeOH:H2O 2:2:1). It enabled the detection of more than 30,000 features from each sample type, with the highest number of uniquely detected features by RPLC in ESI positive mode and by HILIC in ESI negative mode. Therefore, we conclude that when time and sample are limited, the maximum amount of biological information related to lipid and central carbon metabolism can be acquired by combining RPLC ESI positive and HILIC ESI negative mode analysis.
humanized_abstract: Integration of reversed-phase liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry for analysis of microbial metabolomes Although all ‘omic analyses are designed to obtain as broad information as possible about the components of a sample, metabolomics, more than any other ‘omic approach to the comprehensive analysis of bio-molecules has been compromised by a potentially vast number of bio-molecules to be measured. Many approaches to the separation of these bio-molecules have been investigated but, for quantitative analysis of the entire bio-mixture by mass spectrometry, these have introduced bias either to lipophilic or to hydrophilic components. Use of reversed-phase liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (RPLC/MS) is biased toward lipophilic components whereas hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (HILIC/MS) is biased toward hydrophilic components. In this study an aminopropyl column was used for HILIC/MS analysis and optimization was based on the use of eluents with different mobile phase additives and pH and the repeatability of this approach was investigated. When the untargeted analysis of a perturbated bacterial metabolome was explored, a large number of features were identified by HILIC/MS that corresponded to amino acids, organic acids, phosphorylated sugars and energy currency molecules in central carbon metabolism which were not retained under RPLC conditions. Thus, the power of combined approach of RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS analyses was demonstrated with bacterial cells, human plasma and cancer cells as biological samples that were analyzed using RPLC/HILIC with ESI positive and negative modes. Under optimized RPLC conditions that retained the lipid components, the chromatographic eluent was rich in 80% organic solvent (ACN:MeOH:H2O 2:2:1). More than 30,000 features were detected from each of the bio-samples. More features were detected using RPLC/ESI positive mode and more features were detected with HILIC/MS in ESI negative mode than in positive mode.
